symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden severe unilateral flank pain radiating to the groin/testicle

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Classic radiation pattern following the ureteric course as the stone migrates distally.

symptoms.symptom_name: Extreme restlessness, constantly pacing and unable to find a comfortable position

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Visceral colicky pain classically causes restlessness, in contrast to the stillness of peritoneal pain.
Appendicitis patients classically lie still to avoid peritoneal irritation, the opposite of this presentation.
Mucosal trauma from a passing stone commonly causes visible haematuria.
Fever is a hallmark of an infected, obstructed system; its absence is reassuring against this more dangerous variant.
Ureteric colic pain is often severe enough to require parenteral analgesia; failure of oral NSAIDs is common and does not change the diagnosis but supports the need for urgent in-person analgesia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Unilateral costovertebral angle tenderness, absence of peritonism on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Supports a renal/ureteric source and helps exclude a peritoneal surgical process; clinician-only finding sealed from telehealth chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Radio-opaque calculus within the right urinary tract with proximal hydroureter on non-contrast CT KUB

investigation_details.expected_result: Radio-opaque calculus within the right urinary tract with proximal hydroureter
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Diagnosis and stone size/location unconfirmed; telehealth cannot obtain this and should recommend it occur as part of the urgent in-person review.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Definitive imaging confirmation of a ureteric calculus and its location.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Radiating flank-to-groin pain, extreme restlessness, macroscopic haematuria, and absence of fever together support uncomplicated renal colic, warranting urgent same-day analgesia and review rather than emergency ambulance dispatch, provided explicit safety-netting for fever/rigors is given.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line imaging to confirm the calculus, its size, and location, and to exclude hydronephrosis/obstruction requiring urgent intervention.
Screens for infection and assesses renal function, particularly important if bilateral or solitary kidney.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Emergency ambulance/000 dispatch as the default first action for all renal colic
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not appropriate as a blanket response for an afebrile, otherwise uncomplicated presentation; urgent in-person review for analgesia is proportionate, reserving 000/ED for red flags such as fever, anuria, or refractory pain.
